column1: Mesopotamian Sacred Tree: Stylized trees on cylinder seals and palace reliefs symbolize divine order and cosmic axis, possibly influencing later Jewish and Kabbalistic imagery.
Egyptian Djed Pillar: Symbol of stability and the backbone of Osiris, the Djed is a vertical axis motif, sometimes merged with tree symbolism.
Asherah Tree/Pole: Sacred tree or wooden pole associated with the goddess Asherah in Canaanite and early Israelite religion; debated as a precursor to the Tree of Life.
Early Jewish Mysticism (Sefer Yetzirah, Hekhalot): Introduction of ten Sefirot as primordial numbers or emanations, not yet a tree diagram6.

Medieval Kabbalah (Bahir, Zohar): In 12th–13th century Provence and Spain, the Sefirot become divine attributes and are arranged in the Tree of Life structure. The Bahir is the first major text with Sefirot as divine qualities; the Zohar formalizes the system and mystical cosmology2346.

Safed Renaissance/Lurianic Kabbalah: In 16th-century Safed, Moses Cordovero systematizes earlier Kabbalah; Isaac Luria introduces new doctrines (Tzimtzum, Shevirat ha-Kelim, Tikkun Olam), and the Tree of Life becomes a dynamic map of cosmic repair and mystical ascent156.

Hasidism and Modern Kabbalah: Hasidism (18th c.) popularizes Kabbalah, focusing on the Sefirot as a path for personal spirituality. In the modern era, Kabbalah is studied historically and adapted into Western esoteric traditions (Hermetic Qabalah)6.
